era_context: Continuation of the High Middle Ages in Europe; the Second Crusade is still decades away (1147–1149). The Jin Dynasty consolidates control of northern China after the Jin-Song war. The Seljuk Empire under Masud I is at its height. Islamic civilization in Al-Andalus and the eastern Islamic world continues to flourish.

events.title: Henry V of England dies; succession crisis and civil war looms
events.description: King Henry I of England (not Henry V; the numbering is a correction) died on 1 December 1135, but the events leading to succession were already in motion in 1133. Henry I had designated his daughter Matilda as his heir, but this was contested by his nephew Stephen of Blois. The year 1133 saw increasing tension at court and among the Norman nobility over the succession question. Henry I was aging and had no legitimate male heir, which created uncertainty about the future of the Norman kingdom.

sources.name: Anglo-Saxon Chronicle and related English chronicles

events.certainty_note: The succession crisis is well-documented, but specific events in 1133 are less precisely dated. The crisis came to a head with Henry I's death in 1135, but tensions were building in the years before.

events.title: Seljuk military campaigns in Anatolia and Syria
events.description: Sultan Masud I of the Seljuk Empire continued military campaigns to consolidate Seljuk control over Anatolia and to contest Crusader and Armenian principalities in Syria and Cilicia. These campaigns were part of the ongoing struggle for territorial control in the eastern Mediterranean. The Seljuks were at the height of their power during Masud I's reign (1031–1063 for his father; Masud I r. 1055–1092, but his successors continued his policies).
key_figures: Masud I (Seljuk Sultan, or his successors)
events.certainty_note: Seljuk military activity in this region during the 1130s is attested, but specific campaigns in 1133 are not precisely dated in surviving sources. The broader context of Seljuk expansion is well-established.

events.title: Song Dynasty consolidation under Emperor Gaozong
events.description: Emperor Gaozong of Song continued to consolidate power and rebuild the Song state after the Jin invasion and the loss of northern territories. Administrative reforms, military reorganization, and economic recovery were ongoing. The Song court was establishing itself in the south, and Gaozong was working to stabilize the dynasty and prevent further Jin expansion.

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian and Song official histories

events.certainty_note: Song Dynasty records are extensive, but specific events in 1133 are documented in official histories compiled after the fact.

events.title: Abelard and Heloise correspondence and intellectual influence
events.description: Peter Abelard, the renowned scholastic philosopher and theologian, was still alive in 1133 (he died in 1142). His controversial teachings on logic, theology, and ethics were shaping European intellectual discourse. His relationship with Heloise and their correspondence were becoming known in intellectual circles. Abelard's method of dialectical reasoning (Sic et Non) was influencing the development of scholasticism. His work represented a new approach to reconciling faith and reason.

sources.name: Abelard's own works (Sic et Non, Theologia, etc.)

events.certainty_note: Abelard's intellectual activity and influence in 1133 are well-documented through his own writings and correspondence. His life and work are extensively attested.

events.title: Anund Jacob of Sweden dies; succession and political transition
events.description: King Anund Jacob of Sweden died in 1134 (or possibly 1133, sources vary), marking the end of a reign that had seen the consolidation of Swedish power and the expansion of Christian influence. His death led to succession disputes and the rise of his nephew Magnus I. The transition reflected broader changes in Scandinavian politics and the increasing influence of the Christian church in the region.
events.certainty_note: The date of Anund Jacob's death is given as 1134 in most sources, but some sources place it in 1133. The exact year is uncertain due to the limited precision of Scandinavian records from this period.

disconfirming_evidence: The characterization of Abelard as a purely 'rational' or 'proto-modern' thinker is sometimes overstated. His work was deeply rooted in theological concerns and the authority of the Church; his dialectical method was meant to reconcile faith and reason, not to replace faith with reason. Modern scholarship has also questioned some aspects of the Abelard-Heloise correspondence, with some scholars suggesting that later editors may have altered or embellished the letters.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1133 is similar to 1134: strong for European ecclesiastical and political history, good for Song Dynasty China, weaker for the Islamic world and Scandinavia, and essentially absent for most of Africa and the Americas. The sources are heavily weighted toward elite institutions and literate cultures. The succession crisis in England is well-documented because it was of great concern to the Norman nobility and the Church, but the lives of common people are largely invisible in the surviving records.

graph_edges.note: The succession tensions of 1133 led directly to the civil war following Stephen's seizure of the throne in 1135
Abelard's intellectual work in 1133 continued until his death in 1142
Anund Jacob's death occurred around 1133–1134; sources vary on the exact year
